Playing games in class made me miss what the OSI 7-layer model is

Even though I'd started computer classes since elementary school, back then I treated lab computers as gaming consoles — class time was almost entirely gaming, rarely listening. It wasn't until middle / high school, prepping for entrance exams, that I started actually paying attention.
"Everyone, today we're learning the 7-layer network architecture." The teacher wrote it on the board and started explaining.
Usually before class, the teacher would "
" of all classroom computers, syncing everyone's screen to the same image — so no one could secretly game. The software was called "Network Classroom".
"The 7-layer network architecture is a layered model that helps us understand the process of network communication." She switched slides while explaining.
Network 7-layer architecture? Sorry, I only knew "1-layer gaming".
"Hey, XXX, wanna play Nobunaga?" I asked the classmate next to me.
"Wait, the teacher controls your computers? How can you still game?"
Right — back then we really couldn't game. But I'd discovered a trick to escape the teacher's control — just
the computer, the control lifts, and then you can do anything.
The trick is simple: open programs that pop "
" prompts on shutdown — Word, Excel, PowerPoint all work.
When Windows shuts down, it tries to close all programs — including "
If a program pops "Force close?", you select "No" — Network Classroom gets killed but the computer doesn't actually shut down. The teacher's control is lifted — but
good kids, don't try this at home

Is there a downside? Of course. One time the teacher assigned homework at the end of class, but I'd already killed Network Classroom — I had no idea what the assignment was. The only way to restore Network Classroom was to reboot.
Earlier chapters covered email, instant messengers, games — all tied to one core concept: "
To help understand network operation and architecture, let's introduce the "
". Don't get it wrong — this isn't Dragon Ball, no Goku, just packed with network protocols.

In short, the OSI model (Open Systems Interconnection Model) is a reference architecture for network communication. It splits data transmission into 7 layers, making it easier to understand how networking works, and helping different systems interoperate and be maintained.
— real-world protocols (like TCP/IP) aren't strictly 7-layered. But OSI remains an important reference framework for understanding networking, especially for troubleshooting and analysis.
In short, OSI defines network hardware and software as 7 standard layers:
The TCP/IP we actually use is a 4-layer model — a merged version of OSI:
Later it turned out 5 layers are more reasonable for real situations:
"Wait... so many models. What are we actually learning?"
You might wonder: "Why not just teach TCP/IP?"

Simple. OSI is the theoretical model; TCP/IP is the actual implementation. The difference: OSI splits networking more finely, so it's better for analysis and troubleshooting. That's why we'll focus on the OSI 7-layer model.
First, OSI's 7 layers go from bottom (1) to top (7) — Physical layer to Application layer. But we usually explain top-down from layer 7 (Application), since it's closest to the user:
In OSI, higher layers are closer to the user; lower layers are closer to hardware. Each layer has specific functions. To help understanding, here's a scenario:
, and presses Enter. The browser displays Google's homepage."
Xiaoming uses Chrome to send "I want to open google.com" via HTTP — that's your URL request.
the browser converts the request into a computer-understandable format, then encodes / compresses / encrypts (e.g., establishing HTTPS via TLS/SSL).
the browser establishes and maintains a "
" with Google's server, ensuring both sides can continue dialogue. Like saying hi: "I'm Xiaoming, starting communication with you!"
the browser splits data into segments, handed to TCP. TCP adds sequence numbers and error checks, ensuring complete delivery. Even just a request message gets reliably delivered.
each segment gets an IP header — source IP (Xiaoming's computer) and destination IP (Google's server). Network layer uses routers to determine the best path, delivering packets to the right place.
packets get wrapped into Frames, with source and destination MAC addresses added. This layer handles point-to-point transmission, e.g.: "Xiaoming's computer → home Wi-Fi AP → router".
the bottom — data converted to electrical signals (cable), light signals (fiber), or radio waves (Wi-Fi), actually sending 0s and 1s.
Then Google's server receives the request, processes it through the same 7 layers, and returns Google's homepage to Xiaoming's browser, which assembles and renders it on screen.
The OSI flow during transmission: the user side processes from layer 7 down to layer 1, then sends over the network. The server receives and processes from layer 1 up to layer 7.

How does Google reply to Xiaoming? Same flow, just in reverse — Google starts at layer 7 down to layer 1, sends over the network. Xiaoming's computer receives, processes layer 1 up to layer 7, and renders in browser.
Summary of layer names, functions, and common protocols/apps:
User-network interaction interface, providing application services
HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, Telnet, Web, Email
Manages session connection, dialogue control, establishing / maintaining / ending connections
Segmented transmission, error control, flow control, retransmission
Routing, IP address handling, packet transmission, cross-network communication
Encapsulates data frames, error detection, MAC management, same-segment transmission
Ethernet, ARP, PPP, Switch, Wi-Fi MAC layer
Actual medium transmission — converting voltage, EM waves, light signals into 0s and 1s
RJ45, fiber, coaxial cable, radio waves, Hub, Repeater
When I started job interviewing out of school, I genuinely got asked about OSI. My face was probably:
It'd been years since I'd touched this. For the interview, I dug out my old notes and re-reviewed OSI from scratch XD.
